html:
Gaming Realms PLC, RNE1.F / GMR.L

Gaming Realms is still being valued like an illiquid AIM gambling small-cap, but the live facts point to a much better business: an asset-light B2B game IP licensor with no player balance-sheet risk, no sportsbook hold volatility, no customer acquisition burn, no debt, and a growing buyback. The core asset is Slingo, a repeatable game mechanic that operators can plug into regulated online casinos and pair with major brands.

The March 2026 FY25 results were not a vague “growth story.” Revenue rose 10% to £31.4m, licensing revenue rose 13% to £27.6m, adjusted EBITDA rose 15% to £15.0m, and adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 48%. North America is now 63% of content licensing revenue, unique licensing players rose 22%, and the company ended 2025 with £17.8m cash and no borrowings.

The market’s mistake is treating this as a casino operator. It is closer to a niche software royalty stream. FanDuel, DraftKings, BetMGM, Bet365, BCLC, Hard Rock and others own the customer relationship, regulatory burden, marketing spend and betting economics. Gaming Realms supplies content and collects economics when that content performs. That is a much cleaner model than the label implies.

The forward setup is better than the backward multiple suggests. FY25 contained UK staking-limit disruption, heavier development investment, currency drag, and still produced record EBITDA. By year-end, UK revenue had recovered to previous levels after a new Slingo tool was built for the changed UK rules. Q1 2026 started with content licensing revenue 8% ahead year over year, or 10% constant currency. That is not explosive, but it is enough when the business is already near 50% EBITDA margin and buying back stock.

The underrated part is that Gaming Realms is becoming less single-product than the market thinks. Slingo remains the profit engine, but 2025 brought 12 proprietary game releases, 8 bespoke operator or market-specific adaptations, the Lucky Lunar second internal studio, a patent-pending Lock & Slingo mechanic, a larger third-party distribution catalogue, and expanded branded collaborations. The Light & Wonder extension for Slingo versions of 88 Fortunes and Huff N’ More Puff matters because those brands already resonate in North America. Gaming Realms is using outside IP to make Slingo more familiar, not just relying on the legacy Slingo name.

US iGaming remains the torque. The AGA’s State of the States 2026 shows 2025 US iGaming revenue up 27.6% to $10.73bn across only seven legal online casino states. Pennsylvania, Michigan and New Jersey still dominate, but the key point is that the existing regulated footprint is not mature. It is still compounding fast before large states like New York, Illinois or Maryland have legalized full online casino.

Nearer-term, Gaming Realms has concrete market openings. Maine legalized tribal online gambling in January 2026, with the governor allowing LD 1164 to become law, and Gaming Realms has already cited Maine as a future regulated target. Alberta is even more direct: Alberta’s regulated iGaming market is expected to launch July 13, 2026, and Gaming Realms disclosed it had received a conditional iGaming Services Provider license there. These are not hypothetical TAM slides. They are new regulated markets being prepared now.

Valuation is the punchline. The latest May 18 German quotes are messy because RNE1.F is illiquid, with Frankfurt around €0.34 and L&S around €0.367, while the primary London line traded around 31.8p. Using the primary line, the equity value is roughly £87m. After post-year-end buybacks, voting shares are down to 273.8m as of the latest May 18 buyback RNS. Current net cash is probably still around £15m to £17m after buybacks and operating cash generation, putting enterprise value around £70m to £72m.

That is about 4.7x FY25 adjusted EBITDA and roughly 4.0x to 4.3x a sensible 2026 EBITDA estimate of £16.5m to £18m. On forward net income, after normalized tax and the lower share count, the stock is around 10x to 12x earnings. On real free cash flow after capitalized development spend, the valuation is less optically ridiculous but still cheap: roughly 7x to 10x forward EV/FCF for a debt-free content licensor still expanding geographically.

The buyback changes the capital allocation story. This is not idle net cash being hoarded. The company completed the remaining £3.2m of its first £6m program in Q1 2026, then launched another £5m authorization. At these prices, that is highly accretive. The company is retiring shares while the market still prices it as a low-quality gambling microcap.

The main risk is not financial distress. It is product relevance. If Slingo loses lobby placement, if major operators decide the mechanic has aged, or if branded Slingo variants fail to refresh engagement, the margin structure cuts both ways. Regulatory risk is also real, especially because online casino legalization can be politically fragile. The other honest weakness is free cash conversion: Gaming Realms capitalizes meaningful development spend, so EBITDA should not be treated as pure cash flow. Finally, the Frankfurt line is not where serious liquidity lives; the London AIM line is the real market.

The best version of the thesis is simple: the stock does not need heroic legalization to work. It needs mid-teens licensing growth, continued North American mix shift, new market rollouts in Alberta/Maine/Latin America/Africa, and the buyback continuing below intrinsic value. If that happens, the market will not keep valuing a 48% EBITDA-margin, debt-free, regulated-market content licensor at 4x forward EBITDA forever.

Bear case: 22p London / about €0.26 RNE1.F. Slingo growth stalls, brand licensing proves lumpy, UK-style restrictions spread, and EBITDA falls back toward £13m. Apply only 3.5x EBITDA plus reduced net cash. From today’s roughly €0.37, downside is about 30%.

Base case: 70p London / about €0.82 RNE1.F. Licensing grows low-to-mid teens, 2027 EBITDA reaches about £20m, buybacks reduce shares, and the market applies 8.5x EBITDA plus net cash. Upside is about 120%.

Bull case: 125p London / about €1.46 RNE1.F. US/Canada market openings accelerate, branded Slingo titles keep working, Lucky Lunar and third-party distribution add a second growth lane, and a strategic buyer or public market rerating values £25m to £27m of EBITDA at 12x plus cash. Upside is about 300%.
